title: Getting started with suest
text: suest() combines two fitted models calculates joint covariance matrix. suest_newdata() stacks two estimation samples model’s effects can averaged sample. Standard marginaleffects functions—including predictions(), avg_comparisons(), avg_slopes(), hypotheses(), plotting functions—work combined object.

title: Getting started with suest
code: remotes::install_github("tdmize/suest") library(suest) library(marginaleffects)

title: Getting started with suest
text: final command tests difference two effects directly. compare whether one effect statistically significant .
code: dat <- mtcars dat$am <- factor(dat$am)  base <- glm(am ~ wt, family = binomial("logit"), data = dat) adjusted <- glm(am ~ wt + hp, family = binomial("logit"), data = dat) combined <- suest(base, adjusted, model_names = c("Base", "Adjusted")) combined #> Seemingly Unrelated Estimation #> Models: Base + Adjusted  #> Model types: Base=logit, Adjusted=logit  #> Model engines: Base=stats::glm, Adjusted=stats::glm  #> Comparison scale: predicted probabilities  #> Observations: Base=32, Adjusted=32  #> Overlapping observations: 32  #> Union observations: 32  #> Parameters: 5  effects <- avg_comparisons(combined, variables = "wt", newdata = dat) effects #>  #>     Group Estimate Std. Error     z Pr(>|z|)     S  2.5 % 97.5 % #>  Adjusted   -0.355     0.0184 -19.3   <0.001 273.3 -0.391 -0.319 #>  Base       -0.277     0.0232 -11.9   <0.001 106.5 -0.322 -0.231 #>  #> Term: wt #> Type: response #> Comparison: +1 hypotheses(effects, hypothesis = difference ~ revpairwise) #>  #>           Hypothesis Estimate Std. Error     z Pr(>|z|)    S  2.5 %  97.5 % #>  (Adjusted) - (Base)  -0.0779     0.0141 -5.54   <0.001 25.0 -0.105 -0.0503

title: Getting started with suest
text: Models may use identical, partially overlapping, disjoint samples. Supported cross-family comparisons logit–probit, logit–linear, probit–linear, Poisson–negative binomial, ordered logit–multinomial logit. Nonunit weights offsets yet supported. Bias-reduced, adjusted-score, Firth, penalized GLM fits rejected estimating equations differ ordinary maximum-likelihood GLM scores.

title: Getting started with suest
text: examples use public replication data mecompare Stata command. data downloaded reused .
code: library(haven)  ah <- zap_labels(read_dta("https://tdmize.github.io/data/data/ah4_cme.dta")) gss <- zap_labels(read_dta("https://tdmize.github.io/data/data/gss_cme.dta"))  factorize <- function(data, variables) {   data[variables] <- lapply(data[variables], factor)   data }

what: Ex 6.1 - Marginal effects to summarize curvilinear relationships and test mediation
title: Getting started with suest
text: example uses linear regression model nonlinear effect represented polynomial: income income squared included model. test whether adding mediator, job satisfaction, reduces average effect income, suggest mediation. example also shows request custom amount change continuous predictor—case, one-standard-deviation increase income. Example interpretation effect income: model 1, marginal effect -0.816, indicating standard-deviation increase income associated 0.8 fewer depressive symptoms. effect reduced -0.648 accounting job satisfaction. direct cross-model test shows accounting job satisfaction decreases average effect income 0.168, statistically significant reduction (p<.001p < .001) consistent mediation.
code: vars61 <- c("depsympB", "income", "inc10", "age", "woman", "race", "college", "jobsat") dat61 <- ah[complete.cases(ah[vars61]), ] stopifnot(nrow(dat61) == 4307) nrow(dat61) #> [1] 4307 dat61 <- factorize(dat61, c("woman", "race", "jobsat"))  base61 <- lm(depsympB ~ income + I(income^2) + age + woman + race, data = dat61) mediator61 <- lm(depsympB ~ income + I(income^2) + age + woman + race + jobsat,                  data = dat61) fit61 <- suest(base61, mediator61, model_names = c("Base", "Mediator"))  effects61 <- avg_comparisons(fit61,   variables = list(income = sd(dat61$income)), newdata = dat61) effects61 #>  #>     Group Estimate Std. Error      z Pr(>|z|)    S  2.5 % 97.5 % #>  Base       -0.816     0.0756 -10.80   <0.001 87.8 -0.964 -0.668 #>  Mediator   -0.648     0.0739  -8.77   <0.001 59.0 -0.793 -0.503 #>  #> Term: income #> Type: response #> Comparison: +27.2108562966562 hypotheses(effects61, hypothesis = difference ~ revpairwise) #>  #>           Hypothesis Estimate Std. Error     z Pr(>|z|)    S  2.5 % 97.5 % #>  (Base) - (Mediator)   -0.168     0.0222 -7.59   <0.001 44.8 -0.212 -0.125

what: Ex 6.2 - Comparing marginal effects across nested logit models
title: Getting started with suest
text: example uses binary logit models examines effect college changes additional variables added model. Example interpretation effect college: average, probability happy 0.072 higher people college degree without one (p<.001p < .001). Model 2 adds demographic controls, reducing average marginal effect college 0.060, remains statistically significant. direct test shows adding controls decreases effect college 0.012 (p<.01p < .01).
code: vars62 <- c("vhappy", "college", "wages", "occprest", "age", "married",             "parent", "woman", "conserv", "reltrad", "year", "employed") dat62 <- subset(gss, year >= 2000 & employed == 1) dat62 <- dat62[complete.cases(dat62[vars62]), ] stopifnot(nrow(dat62) == 9216) nrow(dat62) #> [1] 9216 dat62 <- factorize(dat62, c("college", "married", "parent", "woman", "conserv",                             "reltrad", "year"))  model62a <- glm(vhappy ~ college, family = binomial("logit"), data = dat62) model62b <- glm(vhappy ~ college + married + parent + woman + conserv + reltrad +                   year + age + I(age^2), family = binomial("logit"), data = dat62) fit62 <- suest(model62a, model62b, model_names = c("Model 1", "Model 2"))  effects62 <- avg_comparisons(fit62, variables = "college", newdata = dat62) effects62 #>  #>    Group Estimate Std. Error    z Pr(>|z|)    S  2.5 % 97.5 % #>  Model 1   0.0718     0.0103 6.95   <0.001 38.0 0.0516 0.0921 #>  Model 2   0.0599     0.0105 5.69   <0.001 26.2 0.0393 0.0806 #>  #> Term: college #> Type: response #> Comparison: 1 - 0 hypotheses(effects62, hypothesis = difference ~ revpairwise) #>  #>             Hypothesis Estimate Std. Error    z Pr(>|z|)   S 2.5 % 97.5 % #>  (Model 1) - (Model 2)   0.0119    0.00402 2.95  0.00313 8.3 0.004 0.0198

what: Ex 6.3 - Comparing marginal effects using alternative predictors
title: Getting started with suest
text: example uses two binary logit models alternative predictors. models use two different ways measure sexuality: sexual behavior (sexbehav) one model sexual identity (sexident) . predictor appears one component model, marginaleffects reports zero NA structurally absent effects. nonzero rows give effects operationalization. Example interpretations: contrasts heterosexual gay/lesbian respondents bisexual gay/lesbian respondents differ significantly across two models. However, difference heterosexual bisexual respondents significantly larger sexuality measured using identity measured using behavior.
code: vars63 <- c("samesexB", "sexident", "sexbehav", "college",             "woman", "race", "age", "year") dat63 <- gss[complete.cases(gss[vars63]), ] stopifnot(nrow(dat63) == 4921) nrow(dat63) #> [1] 4921 dat63 <- factorize(dat63, c("sexident", "sexbehav", "college", "woman", "race", "year"))  behavior63 <- glm(samesexB ~ sexbehav + woman + college + age + race + year,                   family = binomial("logit"), data = dat63) identity63 <- glm(samesexB ~ sexident + woman + college + age + race + year,                   family = binomial("logit"), data = dat63) fit63 <- suest(behavior63, identity63, model_names = c("Behavior", "Identity"))  effects63 <- avg_comparisons(fit63,   variables = list(sexbehav = "reference", sexident = "reference"), newdata = dat63) effects63 #>  #>      Term    Group Contrast Estimate Std. Error      z Pr(>|z|)     S  2.5 %  97.5 % #>  sexbehav Behavior    2 - 1  -0.0972     0.0235  -4.14   <0.001  14.8 -0.143 -0.0511 #>  sexbehav Behavior    3 - 1  -0.3620     0.0384  -9.43   <0.001  67.7 -0.437 -0.2867 #>  sexbehav Identity    2 - 1   0.0000         NA     NA       NA    NA     NA      NA #>  sexbehav Identity    3 - 1   0.0000         NA     NA       NA    NA     NA      NA #>  sexident Behavior    2 - 1   0.0000         NA     NA       NA    NA     NA      NA #>  sexident Behavior    3 - 1   0.0000         NA     NA       NA    NA     NA      NA #>  sexident Identity    2 - 1  -0.2741     0.0415  -6.61   <0.001  34.6 -0.355 -0.1928 #>  sexident Identity    3 - 1  -0.4275     0.0337 -12.67   <0.001 119.8 -0.494 -0.3614 #>  #> Type: response alternative_predictor_hypothesis <- function(x) {   group <- as.character(x$group)   term <- as.character(x$term)   behavior <- x$estimate[term == "sexbehav" & group == "Behavior"]   identity <- x$estimate[term == "sexident" & group == "Identity"]    data.frame(     term = c("Behavior bisexual - Identity bisexual",              "Behavior gay - Identity gay",              "(Behavior gay - bisexual) - (Identity gay - bisexual)"),     estimate = c(behavior[1] - identity[1], behavior[2] - identity[2],                  (behavior[2] - behavior[1]) - (identity[2] - identity[1]))) }  avg_comparisons(fit63,   variables = list(sexbehav = "reference", sexident = "reference"),   newdata = dat63, hypothesis = alternative_predictor_hypothesis) #>  #>                                                   Term Estimate Std. Error     z Pr(>|z|)    S #>  Behavior bisexual - Identity bisexual                   0.1769     0.0425  4.16   <0.001 15.0 #>  Behavior gay - Identity gay                             0.0655     0.0406  1.61   0.1063  3.2 #>  (Behavior gay - bisexual) - (Identity gay - bisexual)  -0.1114     0.0587 -1.90   0.0578  4.1 #>    2.5 %  97.5 % #>   0.0936 0.26023 #>  -0.0140 0.14507 #>  -0.2264 0.00368 #>  #> Type: response

what: Ex 6.4 - Comparing marginal effects across different outcomes
title: Getting started with suest
text: example compares effects across different dependent variables. Two count outcomes—poor mental-health days poor physical-health days—estimated using negative-binomial models, effects calculated predicted-rate scale. Effects cross-model comparisons calculated predictors. reproduce mecompare Stata example exactly, common sample also excludes observations missing reltrad, even though reltrad included either displayed model. Example interpretations: Women report 0.99 poor mental-health days 0.77 poor physical-health days per month men. Although effect gender 0.22 larger mental health, cross-outcome difference statistically significant. married significantly reduces poor mental-health days 1.01, whereas effect poor physical-health days statistically significant. direct comparison shows effect marriage significantly larger mental health physical health (cross-model difference = -0.851, p<.01p < .01). Similarly, effect age differs significantly across outcomes: aging associated fewer poor mental-health days poor physical-health days.
code: vars64 <- c("mntlhlth", "physhlth", "woman", "married", "age",             "faminc", "race", "college", "parent", "reltrad") dat64 <- gss[complete.cases(gss[vars64]), ] stopifnot(nrow(dat64) == 5062) nrow(dat64) #> [1] 5062  dat64 <- factorize(dat64, c("woman", "married", "parent", "college", "race", "year"))  mental64 <- MASS::glm.nb(mntlhlth ~ woman + married + parent + college + age +                            faminc + race + year, data = dat64) physical64 <- MASS::glm.nb(physhlth ~ woman + married + parent + college + age +                              faminc + race + year, data = dat64) fit64 <- suest(mental64, physical64, model_names = c("Mental", "Physical"))  variables64 <- list(woman = "reference", married = "reference", parent = "reference",   college = "reference", age = sd(dat64$age), faminc = sd(dat64$faminc),   race = "reference", year = "reference") effects64 <- avg_comparisons(fit64, variables = variables64, newdata = dat64) effects64 #>  #>     Term    Group          Contrast Estimate Std. Error      z Pr(>|z|)    S  2.5 %   97.5 % #>  age     Mental   +13.0755231484462  -0.4623      0.108 -4.294   <0.001 15.8 -0.673 -0.25129 #>  age     Physical +13.0755231484462   0.4912      0.118  4.179   <0.001 15.1  0.261  0.72155 #>  college Mental   1 - 0              -0.8794      0.229 -3.848   <0.001 13.0 -1.327 -0.43145 #>  college Physical 1 - 0              -0.5422      0.189 -2.870   0.0041  7.9 -0.912 -0.17198 #>  faminc  Mental   +35.054788229243   -0.4445      0.118 -3.769   <0.001 12.6 -0.676 -0.21334 #>  faminc  Physical +35.054788229243   -0.3808      0.102 -3.741   <0.001 12.4 -0.580 -0.18125 #>  married Mental   1 - 0              -1.0103      0.230 -4.397   <0.001 16.5 -1.461 -0.55992 #>  married Physical 1 - 0              -0.1591      0.192 -0.827   0.4082  1.3 -0.536  0.21795 #>  parent  Mental   1 - 0               0.2741      0.248  1.107   0.2682  1.9 -0.211  0.75944 #>  parent  Physical 1 - 0              -0.2692      0.219 -1.231   0.2182  2.2 -0.698  0.15931 #>  race    Mental   2 - 1              -1.0159      0.258 -3.941   <0.001 13.6 -1.521 -0.51070 #>  race    Mental   3 - 1              -0.4381      0.356 -1.230   0.2188  2.2 -1.136  0.26022 #>  race    Physical 2 - 1              -0.5308      0.217 -2.443   0.0146  6.1 -0.957 -0.10487 #>  race    Physical 3 - 1               0.1451      0.343  0.423   0.6724  0.6 -0.527  0.81749 #>  woman   Mental   1 - 0               0.9929      0.208  4.774   <0.001 19.1  0.585  1.40062 #>  woman   Physical 1 - 0               0.7734      0.174  4.446   <0.001 16.8  0.432  1.11430 #>  year    Mental   2006 - 2002        -0.9438      0.270 -3.492   <0.001 11.0 -1.474 -0.41408 #>  year    Mental   2010 - 2002        -0.0675      0.318 -0.213   0.8316  0.3 -0.690  0.55484 #>  year    Mental   2014 - 2002        -0.5819      0.302 -1.929   0.0537  4.2 -1.173  0.00922 #>  year    Physical 2006 - 2002        -0.2917      0.225 -1.299   0.1939  2.4 -0.732  0.14839 #>  year    Physical 2010 - 2002         0.3233      0.261  1.240   0.2148  2.2 -0.187  0.83400 #>  year    Physical 2014 - 2002        -0.2286      0.245 -0.933   0.3507  1.5 -0.709  0.25148 #>  #> Type: response  age64 <- avg_comparisons(fit64, variables = list(age = sd(dat64$age)),                          newdata = dat64) hypotheses(age64, hypothesis = difference ~ revpairwise) #>  #>             Hypothesis Estimate Std. Error     z Pr(>|z|)    S 2.5 % 97.5 % #>  (Mental) - (Physical)   -0.953      0.132 -7.22   <0.001 40.8 -1.21 -0.694

what: Ex 6.5 - Comparing marginal effects across different model types (ordinal vs nominal)
title: Getting started with suest
text: example compares effects across two model types: ordinal model nominal model. models otherwise identical, using outcome predictors. also illustrates custom effect continuous predictor: change age 20 30, covariates held means. estimand important. mecompare Stata command compares age 20 age 30 holds model-matrix columns means. averaging ten-year forward change observed ages covariates. distinction especially consequential age enters quadratically. also reproduced exactly newdata = "mean" marginaleffects, uses means numeric variables modes categorical variables. helper supplied suest reproduces Stata’s model-matrix atmeans calculation exactly. View helper code. Example interpretation: someone 20 years old, effect ten-year increase age differs significantly across ordinal nominal models three five outcome categories. example, aging increases probability identifying strong Democrat models, increase significantly larger nominal model. strikingly, effects age identifying strong Republican run opposite directions across two models.
code: vars65 <- c("partyid5", "woman", "edyrs", "age", "parent", "married",             "faminc", "employed", "region4", "year", "race") dat65 <- subset(gss, year >= 2010) dat65 <- dat65[complete.cases(dat65[vars65]), ] stopifnot(nrow(dat65) == 8179) nrow(dat65) #> [1] 8179 dat65 <- factorize(dat65, c("woman", "parent", "married", "race",                             "employed", "region4", "year"))  party_levels <- sort(unique(dat65$partyid5)) party_labels <- c("Strong Democrat", "Democrat", "Independent",                   "Republican", "Strong Republican") dat65$party_ord <- ordered(dat65$partyid5, levels = party_levels, labels = party_labels) dat65$party_nom <- factor(dat65$partyid5, levels = party_levels, labels = party_labels)  ordered65 <- MASS::polr(party_ord ~ age + I(age^2) + woman + edyrs + parent +                           married + race + faminc + employed + region4 + year,                         data = dat65, method = "logistic", Hess = TRUE) nominal65 <- nnet::multinom(party_nom ~ age + I(age^2) + woman + edyrs + parent +                              married + race + faminc + employed + region4 + year,                            data = dat65, Hess = TRUE, trace = FALSE) fit65 <- suest(ordered65, nominal65, model_names = c("Ordered", "Multinomial")) source(system.file("example-code", "example-6-5-atmeans.R", package = "suest")) results65 <- example65_atmeans(fit65, age_lo = 20, age_hi = 30) results65$effects #>             category       model      estimate   std.error      p.value #> 1    Strong Democrat     Ordered  0.0200043395 0.003873903 2.418938e-07 #> 2           Democrat     Ordered  0.0233309158 0.005309554 1.112128e-05 #> 3        Independent     Ordered -0.0030269099 0.000485863 4.665355e-10 #> 4         Republican     Ordered -0.0244327347 0.005230285 2.991588e-06 #> 5  Strong Republican     Ordered -0.0158756107 0.003786173 2.752300e-05 #> 6    Strong Democrat Multinomial  0.0324035507 0.003318210 1.585306e-22 #> 7           Democrat Multinomial -0.0102369435 0.011006328 3.523213e-01 #> 8        Independent Multinomial -0.0004415478 0.010034596 9.649024e-01 #> 9         Republican Multinomial -0.0314658066 0.010558017 2.879885e-03 #> 10 Strong Republican Multinomial  0.0097407472 0.003265056 2.851280e-03 results65$differences #>            category     estimate   std.error      p.value #> 1   Strong Democrat -0.012399211 0.003072737 5.454810e-05 #> 2          Democrat  0.033567859 0.009357678 3.342486e-04 #> 3       Independent -0.002585362 0.010024139 7.964736e-01 #> 4        Republican  0.007033072 0.009022795 4.356981e-01 #> 5 Strong Republican -0.025616358 0.003720332 5.758342e-12

what: Ex 6.6 - Comparing marginal effects across different samples or groups
title: Getting started with suest
text: example compares effects separate models estimated different samples: one sample 1986 another 2016. models otherwise identical, using binary logit outcome predictors. approach can used compare models different groups, men women. R, samples selected subset, suest_newdata() ensures marginal effect averaged appropriate estimation sample. Example interpretation: Conservatives significantly lower predicted probabilities saying government responsible providing health care 1986 (marginal effect = -0.092) 2016 (marginal effect = -0.258). gap conservatives nonconservatives increased time: conservative effect significantly negative 2016 1986 (cross-model difference = 0.166, p<.001p < .001).
code: vars66 <- c("helpsickB", "polviews", "conserv", "faminc", "employed",             "woman", "age", "college", "married", "parent", "race", "year") dat66 <- gss[complete.cases(gss[vars66]), ] dat66 <- factorize(dat66, c("conserv", "employed", "woman", "college",                             "married", "parent", "race"))  model1986 <- glm(helpsickB ~ conserv + faminc + employed + woman + age +                    college + married + parent + race, family = binomial("logit"),                  data = dat66, subset = year == 1986) model2016 <- glm(helpsickB ~ conserv + faminc + employed + woman + age +                    college + married + parent + race, family = binomial("logit"),                  data = dat66, subset = year == 2016) stopifnot(nobs(model1986) == 1254, nobs(model2016) == 1670) c(`1986` = nobs(model1986), `2016` = nobs(model2016)) #> 1986 2016  #> 1254 1670 fit66 <- suest(model1986, model2016, model_names = c("1986", "2016")) fit66 #> Seemingly Unrelated Estimation #> Models: 1986 + 2016  #> Model types: 1986=logit, 2016=logit  #> Model engines: 1986=stats::glm, 2016=stats::glm  #> Comparison scale: predicted probabilities  #> Observations: 1986=1,254, 2016=1,670  #> Overlapping observations: 0  #> Union observations: 2,924  #> Parameters: 22  effects66 <- avg_comparisons(fit66, variables = "conserv",                              newdata = suest_newdata(fit66)) effects66 #>  #>  Group Estimate Std. Error     z Pr(>|z|)    S  2.5 %  97.5 % #>   1986  -0.0917     0.0296  -3.1  0.00193  9.0 -0.150 -0.0337 #>   2016  -0.2582     0.0253 -10.2  < 0.001 78.7 -0.308 -0.2085 #>  #> Term: conserv #> Type: response #> Comparison: 1 - 0 hypotheses(effects66, hypothesis = difference ~ revpairwise) #>  #>       Hypothesis Estimate Std. Error    z Pr(>|z|)    S  2.5 % 97.5 % #>  (1986) - (2016)    0.166     0.0389 4.28   <0.001 15.7 0.0902  0.243

title: Getting started with suest
text: statistical model family fitting engine handled separately. allows comparisons across packages likelihood scores, parameter ordering, observation alignment, prediction scales compatible.

title: Getting started with suest
code: library(glm2)  standard <- glm(am ~ wt + hp, family = binomial("logit"), data = dat) alternative <- glm2(am ~ wt + hp, family = binomial("logit"), data = dat) engine_fit <- suest(standard, alternative, model_names = c("glm", "glm2")) engine_fit #> Seemingly Unrelated Estimation #> Models: glm + glm2  #> Model types: glm=logit, glm2=logit  #> Model engines: glm=stats::glm, glm2=glm2::glm2  #> Comparison scale: predicted probabilities  #> Observations: glm=32, glm2=32  #> Overlapping observations: 32  #> Union observations: 32  #> Parameters: 6 avg_comparisons(engine_fit, variables = "wt", newdata = dat) #>  #>  Group Estimate Std. Error     z Pr(>|z|)     S  2.5 % 97.5 % #>   glm    -0.355     0.0184 -19.3   <0.001 273.3 -0.391 -0.319 #>   glm2   -0.355     0.0184 -19.3   <0.001 273.3 -0.391 -0.319 #>  #> Term: wt #> Type: response #> Comparison: +1

title: Getting started with suest
text: clm() supported proportional-effects ordered logit probit models flexible thresholds scale nominal-effects formula.
code: library(ordinal)  housing <- MASS::housing[rep(seq_len(nrow(MASS::housing)), MASS::housing$Freq),                          c("Sat", "Infl", "Type", "Cont")] rownames(housing) <- NULL housing$Sat <- ordered(housing$Sat, levels = c("Low", "Medium", "High"))  clm_model <- clm(Sat ~ Cont + Infl + Type, data = housing, link = "logit") polr_model <- MASS::polr(Sat ~ Cont + Infl + Type, data = housing,                          method = "logistic", Hess = TRUE) ordinal_fit <- suest(clm_model, polr_model, model_names = c("clm", "polr")) avg_comparisons(ordinal_fit, variables = "Cont", newdata = housing) #>  #>         Group Estimate Std. Error     z Pr(>|z|)    S    2.5 %    97.5 % #>  clm::Low     -0.07504    0.02010 -3.73   <0.001 12.4 -0.11443 -0.035653 #>  clm::Medium  -0.00374    0.00176 -2.13    0.033  4.9 -0.00718 -0.000303 #>  clm::High     0.07878    0.02071  3.80   <0.001 12.8  0.03819  0.119372 #>  polr::Low    -0.07504    0.02010 -3.73   <0.001 12.4 -0.11443 -0.035653 #>  polr::Medium -0.00374    0.00176 -2.13    0.033  4.9 -0.00718 -0.000303 #>  polr::High    0.07878    0.02071  3.80   <0.001 12.8  0.03819  0.119372 #>  #> Term: Cont #> Type: response #> Comparison: High - Low

title: Getting started with suest
text: framework available Stata mecompare Stata command. R package uses marginaleffects predictions effects, mecompare Stata command provides Stata-native workflow class cross-model comparisons.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Trenton D. Mize. Author, maintainer.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Mize, Trenton D., Long Doan, J. Scott Long. 2019. "General Framework Comparing Predictions Marginal Effects Across Models." Sociological Methodology 49(1):152--189. doi:10.1177/0081175019852763.

Scott Long},   journal = {Sociological Methodology},   year = {2019},   volume = {49},   number = {1},   pages = {152--189},   doi = {10.1177/0081175019852763}, }

what: suest for comparing predictions and marginal effects across models
title: suest for comparing predictions and marginal effects across models
text: Full documentation, worked examples, rendered output: tdmize.github.io/suest suest combines two separately fitted regression models one object joint model-robust covariance matrix. combined object works marginaleffects compare predictions marginal effects across models. package implements framework developed : Mize, Trenton D., Long Doan, J. Scott Long. 2019. “General Framework Comparing Predictions Marginal Effects Across Models.” Sociological Methodology 49(1):152–189. doi:10.1177/0081175019852763

title: suest for comparing predictions and marginal effects across models
code: # install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("tdmize/suest")

title: suest for comparing predictions and marginal effects across models
text: suest() accounts cross-model covariance estimates. essential models use overlapping observations.
code: library(suest) library(marginaleffects)  dat <- mtcars dat$am <- factor(dat$am)  base <- glm(am ~ wt, family = binomial("logit"), data = dat) adjusted <- glm(am ~ wt + hp, family = binomial("logit"), data = dat) combined <- suest(base, adjusted, model_names = c("Base", "Adjusted"))  effects <- avg_comparisons(combined, variables = "wt", newdata = dat) effects hypotheses(effects, hypothesis = difference ~ revpairwise)

title: suest for comparing predictions and marginal effects across models
text: linear regression using lm() binary logit probit using glm() glm2::glm2() Poisson regression using glm() glm2::glm2() negative-binomial regression using MASS::glm.nb() ordered logit probit using MASS::polr() ordered logit probit using restricted ordinal::clm() specifications multinomial logit using nnet::multinom() Models may use identical, partially overlapping, disjoint samples. Supported cross-family comparisons logit–probit, logit–linear, probit–linear, Poisson–negative binomial, ordered logit–multinomial logit.

title: suest for comparing predictions and marginal effects across models
text: single Get Started page contains code rendered results six R replications corresponding Examples 6.1–6.6 mecompare Stata command: Marginal effects summarize curvilinear relationships test mediation Comparing marginal effects across nested logit models Comparing marginal effects using alternative predictors Comparing marginal effects across different outcomes Comparing marginal effects across different model types (ordinal vs nominal) Comparing marginal effects across different samples groups also documents cross-engine comparisons using glm2::glm2() ordinal::clm(). Bias-reduced, adjusted-score, Firth, penalized GLM fits, including brglm2::brglmFit(), rejected rather treated incorrectly ordinary maximum-likelihood GLMs.

what: Combine two fitted models with seemingly unrelated estimation — suest
title: Combine two fitted models with seemingly unrelated estimation — suest
text: suest() combines two separately fitted models constructs joint model-robust covariance matrix observation-level score contributions. returned object can passed directly marginaleffects::predictions(), marginaleffects::avg_comparisons(), marginaleffects::avg_slopes(), marginaleffects::hypotheses().

title: Combine two fitted models with seemingly unrelated estimation — suest
code: suest(model1, model2, model_names = NULL)  # S3 method for class 'suest_model' coef(object, ...)  # S3 method for class 'suest_model' vcov(object, ...)  # S3 method for class 'suest_model' nobs(object, ...)  # S3 method for class 'suest_model' print(x, ...)

title: Combine two fitted models with seemingly unrelated estimation — suest
text: model1, model2 Two supported fitted model objects. model_names Optional character vector containing two display names. default, object names supplied call used. object, x "suest_model" object. ... Additional arguments, currently ignored.

title: Combine two fitted models with seemingly unrelated estimation — suest
text: object class "suest_model" containing two fitted models, joint coefficient vector, joint model-robust covariance matrix, sample-alignment information.

title: Combine two fitted models with seemingly unrelated estimation — suest
text: Exactly two models supported. Models can use identical, partially overlapping, completely disjoint samples. model calls refer data source, model-frame row names identify overlapping observations. Models fitted different data objects treated disjoint shared observations inferred safely without identifier. -family comparisons supported listed model types. supported cross-family pairs logit–probit, logit–linear, probit–linear, Poisson–negative binomial, ordered logit–multinomial logit. Negative-binomial models include log(theta) joint parameter vector. Ordered multinomial models use analytic score observed-information calculations stable robust covariance estimation. Offsets, nonunit estimation weights, aliased parameters currently supported. Bias-reduced, adjusted-score, Firth, penalized GLM fits rejected use ordinary maximum-likelihood score equations.

title: Combine two fitted models with seemingly unrelated estimation — suest
text: stats::lm() binary logit probit models stats::glm() glm2::glm2() Poisson log-link models stats::glm() glm2::glm2() negative-binomial log-link models MASS::glm.nb() ordered logit probit models MASS::polr() ordered logit probit models ordinal::clm() flexible thresholds, proportional effects, scale model multinomial logit models nnet::multinom()

title: Combine two fitted models with seemingly unrelated estimation — suest
code: dat <- mtcars dat$am <- factor(dat$am)  model1 <- glm(am ~ wt, family = binomial(), data = dat) model2 <- glm(am ~ wt + hp, family = binomial(), data = dat) fit <- suest(model1, model2, model_names = c("Base", "Adjusted")) fit #> Seemingly Unrelated Estimation #> Models: Base + Adjusted  #> Model types: Base=logit, Adjusted=logit  #> Model engines: Base=stats::glm, Adjusted=stats::glm  #> Comparison scale: predicted probabilities  #> Observations: Base=32, Adjusted=32  #> Overlapping observations: 32  #> Union observations: 32  #> Parameters: 5   effects <- marginaleffects::avg_comparisons(fit, variables = "wt", newdata = dat) marginaleffects::hypotheses(effects, hypothesis = difference ~ revpairwise) #>  #>           Hypothesis Estimate Std. Error     z Pr(>|z|)    S  2.5 %  97.5 % #>  (Adjusted) - (Base)  -0.0779     0.0141 -5.54   <0.001 25.0 -0.105 -0.0503 #>  #>

what: Stack the estimation samples from a SUEST object — suest_newdata
title: Stack the estimation samples from a SUEST object — suest_newdata
text: Creates data frame containing component model's estimation sample. useful two models fitted different samples marginal effects averaged separately within model's observed covariate distribution.

title: Stack the estimation samples from a SUEST object — suest_newdata
code: suest_newdata(object)

title: Stack the estimation samples from a SUEST object — suest_newdata
text: object "suest_model" returned suest().

title: Stack the estimation samples from a SUEST object — suest_newdata
text: data frame component model frames stacked vertically two internal columns, .suest_model .suest_rowid, used route rows correct component model.

title: Stack the estimation samples from a SUEST object — suest_newdata
code: dat <- mtcars dat$am <- factor(dat$am)  model1 <- glm(am ~ wt + hp, family = binomial(), data = dat, subset = cyl == 4) model2 <- glm(am ~ wt + hp, family = binomial(), data = dat, subset = cyl != 4) #> Warning: glm.fit: algorithm did not converge #> Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred fit <- suest(model1, model2, model_names = c("Four cylinders", "Other")) nd <- suest_newdata(fit) marginaleffects::avg_comparisons(fit, variables = "wt", newdata = nd) #>  #>           Group Estimate Std. Error         z Pr(>|z|)    S  2.5 % 97.5 % #>  Four cylinders   -0.596   9.08e-02 -6.57e+00   <0.001 34.2 -0.774 -0.418 #>  Other            -0.238   2.90e-11 -8.20e+09   <0.001  Inf -0.238 -0.238 #>  #> Term: wt #> Type: response #> Comparison: +1 #>

title: suest 0.1.3
text: Corrected Example 6.5 reproduce exact published estimand: age change 20 30 model-matrix columns held means. Added supplied Stata-style atmeans replication helper linked output directly validated numerical acceptance test. Added explicit sample-size checks six paper examples website build fails instead silently displaying results different sample. Matched Example 6.4 sample-selection statement exactly mecompare Stata command example added explicit check displayed result confirming N = 5,062. Simplified continuous-variable comparisons using native marginaleffects numeric forward-contrast syntax. Restored every example’s common-sample restrictions match corresponding mecompare Stata example exactly, including variables used define analysis sample. Revised headings, setup descriptions, interpretations Examples 6.1–6.6 follow language organization mecompare Stata command website, adapted R workflow. Fixed package-level roxygen links converted NAMESPACE manual pages roxygen-managed files. Marked package help topic internal appear missing pkgdown reference topic. Added clean-process website build script updated GitHub workflow use package already installed setup-r-dependencies. Added explicit website dependencies restored exact validated model specifications consolidated Get Started page. Reorganized pkgdown documentation around one comprehensive Get Started page, following structure cleanplots package site. Moved six paper replications alternative-engine documentation onto main page removed separate Articles menu. Enabled code evaluation pkgdown builds example output results appear website, keeping network-dependent chunks unevaluated ordinary package checks. Condensed example code throughout vignette, README, function documentation. Clarified throughout mecompare Stata command. Corrected direct adapter tests call package’s registered suest_model methods directly. public marginaleffects::get_predict() helper normalizes type = "response" native clm objects S3 dispatch therefore appropriate unit-test entry point. Retained direct coefficient-perturbation check ordinal::clm() leaving end--end integration testing avg_comparisons().

title: suest 0.1.2
text: Fixed ordinal::clm() prediction coefficient-replacement dispatch model-engine vectors carry model-name attributes. Added direct tests clm() probability predictions respond coefficient perturbations. Corrected two focused acceptance-test reporting statements treated returned error-message strings condition objects.

title: suest 0.1.1
text: Made acceptance-test runners remove stale global suest() suest_newdata() functions devtools::load_all(). prevents previously sourced standalone implementation masking package code. Added test-runner guard log entry confirming tests use package namespace model-adapter implementation. Added model-adapter layer separates statistical model family package function used fit . Added tested support glm2::glm2() binary logit, binary probit, Poisson models. Added support ordinal::clm() ordered logit ordered probit models flexible thresholds, proportional effects, scale model. Added defensive rejection bias-reduced, adjusted-score, Firth, penalized GLM fits brglm2::brglmFit(). Expanded numerical acceptance suite cover full cross-model comparison--model-family matrix, including nested/mediator comparisons, alternative predictor operationalizations, different outcomes, sex-stratified samples, missing-data sample changes, partially overlapping samples. Added -sample disjoint-sample tests every supported cross-family comparison. Imported stats generics used suest_model S3 methods namespace loads correctly R CMD check pkgdown builds. Updated GitHub Actions checkout steps actions/checkout@v6 GitHub Pages deployment action version 4.8.0. Corrected vignette metadata article unique VignetteIndexEntry. Updated exact-zero covariance tests ignore irrelevant matrix dimnames. Added local package pkgdown preflight script.

title: suest 0.1.0
text: Initial GitHub release. Combines exactly two supported cross-sectional models using joint model-robust covariance matrix. Integrates marginaleffects predictions, comparisons, slopes, hypotheses, plots. Supports identical, partially overlapping, disjoint estimation samples. Includes replication vignettes Examples 6.1–6.6 Mize, Doan, Long (2019).
